evidence_quotes.quote: getting verified by Google is a process that costs the developer money, currently 25$.

paragraphs.text: Google's ban on installing applications from non-verified developers on Android phones is a step more towards Google's monopoly on our digital lives. It will seriously hurt, if not completely destroy, most open-source app initiatives. Countries like Denmark have shown that moving towards sovereign-controlled technologies, by moving some of its government infrastructure to Linux and other open-source tools, will help to maintain control over our own security instead of relying on a foreign brand's judgement, as well as reduce cost of infrastructure and improve technological democracy. Being able to contribute to an open-source app is a great analogy of how democracy should work, open-source as a whole is a great example of democracy in the digital realm. However, if Google bans installing apps from untrusted developers, it means that they have a say on who can publish tools and who can't, which is against digital liberty. It is an OS's job to warn the user what is potentially dangerous, but the user's choice to do what they please with their device. Although not many users are conscious about their digital sovereignty and security (which in of itself is a whole other problem), many security-conscious users choose an Android phone over an iOS one for the amount of customisability and freedom they offer compared to the alternative. This feedback mainly adresses Google's ban, since it is fairly recent and I believe the EU can help to stop it, but of course, Apple allowing to install un-verified apps on their phones would be a great step towards liberty as well. I can personally see, more than just Denmark, a rise overall in open-source, by example with the rising amount of computer users choosing Linux over Windows/MacOS. Notably, I could reference PewDiePie, famous online personality and native EU citizen, inviting his community to switch to Linux and other open-source projects, rejecting capitalist monopolies in favour of people-owned tools; or the school I attend, La Cambre (Brussels), moving from Google's ecosystem to a self-hosted open-source alternative, NextCloud, greatly reducing costs and privacy. On another point, making app development and publishing easier is of course a great help to innovation. Adding more steps to publishing an app will discourage new developpers to make their ideas come to life. Moreover, getting verified by Google is a process that costs the developer money, currently 25$. Overall, I believe that this measure that Google presents as "security" is obviously a way for them to control contents of millions of phones. If only security was in mind, a simple warning that the app the user is about to install comes from an unverified developer would be plenty enough. I, as a European citizen greatly believing in digital sovereignty and open-source apps, hope that the EU will do something about this.
